g.data-narration: Welcome to MPI Timing Workflow - the essential skill for measuring parallel program performance accurately.
g.text: Measuring Performance in MIMD Parallel Programs

g.data-narration: Timing is the foundation of parallel program evaluation. Without accurate measurements, we cannot determine if parallelization delivers real benefits.
g.text: Why Timing Matters in Parallel Programs
Parallel code doesn't automatically mean faster code. Timing quantifies
speedup, efficiency, and reveals hidden overheads like communication.

g.data-narration: Always use wall-clock time for parallel programs - it measures real elapsed time. CPU time only counts work on one processor.
g.text: Real elapsed time from start to end

g.data-narration: MPI_Wtime returns high-resolution wall-clock time as a double in seconds. It's portable across all MPI implementations.
g.text: Returns: Seconds since arbitrary point in past (double)

g.data-narration: MPI_Barrier ensures ALL processes reach the same point before continuing. This prevents timing skew from lazy or fast processes.
g.text: Synchronizes ALL processes - no one proceeds until everyone arrives.
Critical for accurate timing: ensures parallel work truly starts/stops together.

g.data-narration: The standard timing pattern: Barrier → record start → parallel work → Barrier → record end → calculate elapsed time.
Here's the actual MPI code. Notice rank 0 handles timing while all processes synchronize at barriers for accurate measurement.
After the parallel work, another barrier ensures all processes finish before rank 0 records the end time and calculates elapsed time.
This timeline shows the synchronized execution. All processes run in parallel, but barriers ensure timing accuracy.
Parallel programs have overheads: communication latency, synchronization costs, load imbalance, and cache effects all affect timing.
To evaluate parallel performance, we calculate speedup and efficiency. These metrics reveal how well our program utilizes multiple processors.
g.text: After timing, calculate key metrics to evaluate parallel program quality:
How much faster with N processors?

g.data-narration: Speedup = T_sequential / T_parallel. Ideal speedup equals the number of processors. Real speedup is usually less due to overheads.
Efficiency = Speedup / N. 100% efficiency means perfect parallelization. Most real programs achieve 50-90% efficiency.
